items.myth: Early stopping and 'restore best weights' are the same thing.
items.reality: Early stopping decides when to halt; restore best weights decides which checkpoint to keep. You can stop correctly but still deploy the wrong (final) epoch if restore is disabled.
items.example: Keras stops at epoch 60 with restore_best_weights=False — model weights are from epoch 60, but val_loss was lowest at epoch 45.

items.myth: If training stopped early, the model is definitely not overfit.
items.reality: Early stopping reduces overfitting risk but does not guarantee it — wrong architecture, leakage, or a too-small validation set can still yield overfit checkpoints at the 'best' epoch.
items.example: Validation rows leaked into training — early stopping picks the best of still-inflated val scores; locked test set reveals the gap.

items.myth: Patience should always be small to save GPU time.
items.reality: Small patience on noisy validation curves stops before the true peak — trading saved compute for underfit models. Patience should match validation set size and metric variance.
items.example: patience=2 on a 100-row val set stops at epoch 11; val/f1 keeps improving until epoch 19 with patience=12.

items.myth: Early stopping replaces the need for a validation set in other tuning steps.
items.reality: Early stopping consumes the validation signal each epoch — if you also grid-search many hyperparameters on the same validation curve, you still need discipline (nested CV or a fresh validation slice) to avoid overfitting the validation set itself.
items.example: 50 learning rates each early-stopped on the same val split — best combo is overfit to that slice; report on locked test once.
